paragraphs.text: Hello! I have two concerns. On the one hand, I would very much welcome the inclusion of games in this context. Among other things, we have to deal with some Historical elements, which can simply be lost, and no one seems to be doing anything against it. These are just as protective as books, series, films and newspapers; they are only completely missing because they are sometimes purely digital. Especially since these goods are initially considered to be goods, they are now to be sold as a licence in the same way. Dark patterns have often been seen. I believe most recently in Diablo 4 of Activision Blizzard King Entertainment. You have long been aware of the elements that make us acquainted here. Loot boxes the children/persons are supposed to fix and are almost gambling. The anti-gambling law is thus very easily circumvented in many countries without direct payment. Seasonpasses the FOMO (Fear of Missing out) promoten da Ingame Items can only be reached in time investments which are unrealistic. This makes it easier to spend money in order to speed up the process. Similarly, Ingame shops also produce FOMO through item rotation. It has been a problem for a long time and clearly. EAS football matches can be seen superseded by absurdly making a lot of money. These games need at least the highest rating if this should not be actively prevented. We are also aware of some companies (Activision) who have already acquired patents, which is why you are irritating to buy them. Implementation is likely to be a matter of time. Furthermore, software sold as perpetual was also intended to be legally binding. Should software be sold in this way, this should not be retroactively alterable, even in the event of a takeover. Software or abandonware should be made publicly available. A version of the software should always be available from the EU, like me, OR the Internet Archive should be supported as an official source. Software should also be transferable like normal goods. MFG, Alexander
